Testing the method on a SGP CSAPR volume from the same squall line event near 1120 UTC. Using the first 5 elevation scans (0.8-3.5 deg) and performing 100 iterations took ~ 40 sec on my machine. Note that this method is agnostic to the unambiguous range of differential phase (e.g., STAR versus alternate transmission modes; 360 deg versus 180 deg), just so long as the differential phase tendency is to increase with range. 

![test_kdp_csapr](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2049028/13331314/e6cbd490-dbca-11e5-9ad0-98f6e17c3b13.png)

Should we remove (mask) gates in the retrieved fields which were not used, e.g., the excluded gates defined in a `GateFilter`?

Here are individual rays from the base elevation scan. The black dashed horizontal lines correspond to the near and far range BC values in which the retrieved PHI_DP solutions must vary between. The black dashed vertical line corresponds to the range gate of the far BC, i.e., not necessarily the maximum range of CSAPR. PSI_DP values where the significant detection was less than 1 have been masked. Note the spurious far range BC in the 270 deg azimuth panel and associated spike in KDP. This may be due to non-uniform beam filling near the edge of the cloud. Regardless of the cause, as of now there is no sanity check on the far range BC, just thought I'd point that out.

On 05/10/2016 03:40 PM, Mihai Cara wrote:
> From what I see in equations between B1 and B2 in 
> http://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/abs/2002/45/aah3860/aah3860.html 
> ­these are fundamentally the same as eq (2) if we try to solve for 
> alpha and delta. Try to expand l=|r_11*l' + r_12*m' + r_13*n'| and you 
> will get terms such as |cos(phi-phi_0)| and |sin(phi-phi_0)|. The need 
> to develop approximations that work for |phi~phi_0| is a different issue.

In method _rotate_zxz in _SkyRotation:

         theta_f = np.arcsin(sin_theta_i * sin_lat +
                             cos_theta_i * cos_lat * cos_delta)

I don't know whether that's THE problem, but it certainly can give 
inaccurate results.  When the exact value of theta_f is close to pi / 2, 
a small change in the argument to the arcsin function gives a large 
change in the angle, i.e. the derivative of arcsin(x) is infinite at x = 1.

If you do a rotation in 3-D by converting the vector to rectangular 
coordinates, multiplying by a rotation matrix, then converting back to 
spherical coordinates (using the arctan2 function for both angles), 
there won't be any singular points in the transformation.  Also, I 
suspect that the expression for theta_f in _rotate_zxz could be replaced 
by one that uses arctan2 instead of arcsin.

OK, current status is:

- `storage.connection.Connection.get_bucket()` incurs a network hit, and raises a `NotFound` on a miss.

- `storage.connection.Connection.create_bucket()` incurs a network hit, and raises (`Conflict`, i.e. HTTP 409 status?) if the bucket exists already (API docs don't define error responses, and furthermore *Carthago delenda est*).

- `storage.bucket.Bucket.get_blob()` incurs a network hit, and converts the `NotFound` on a miss to a `None`.

- `storage.bucket.Bucket.new_blob()` does *not* incur a network hit.

@jgeewax: IIUC, you would like `Connection.__getitem__()` to return a new `Bucket` instance, *without* the network hit, and have `Bucket.exists()` do that lookup if.  Likewise, you would like `Bucket.__getitem__()` to return a new `Blob` instance, again *without* the network hit, and have `Blob.exists()` do that lookup.

In this universe, I think `Connection.get_bucket()`, `Connection.create_bucket()`, `Bucket.get_blob()`, and  `Bucket.new_blob()` would all disappear.  Applications that wanted to ensure that a bucket or blob did or did not already exist would just call `exits()` and handle it themselves.

This mode is somewhat counter-intuitive for Python programmers, who might reasonably expect `__getitem__()` to raise a `KeyError` on a miss.  Unfortunately, we have to choose:  a) do what I just proposed, and be less "Pythonic"; b) incur the network lookup (more "Pythonic" but worse at scale), or c) work out a mode for caching the buckets / blobs locally, and live with the pain / complexity of trying to do cache invalidation correctly.

Sachin, The code below worked for me with no duplicates. I have a vague subscription that your error handling at the very bottom might be to blame for your results: the underlying code occasionally surfaces exceptions that come from closed connections. The code will rebuild the connection automatically. You, however, close the client and rebuild it -- which stops all renewals. Try catching only specific exception types maybe? 
import google.cloud.pubsub_v1 as pubsub
from google.cloud import exceptions
import time
import logging,logging.handlers
from datetime import datetime
import sys,os

subscriber = pubsub.SubscriberClient()
sname = 'projects/google.com:kir-learns-cloud/subscriptions/sachin'

def timestamp():
  return time.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S', time.localtime(time.time()))

def create_topic(project_id, topic_name):
    publisher = pubsub.PublisherClient()
    topic_path = publisher.topic_path(project_id, topic_name)
    publish_future = publisher.publish(topic_path, 'Published at: %s ' % timestamp())
    # wait for the publish to succeed
    logging.warn("PUB : %s at %s", publish_future.result(), timestamp())

def callback(pubsub_message):
    logging.warn("PULL: %s %s at %s", pubsub_message.message_id, pubsub_message.data, timestamp())
    time.sleep(60*12)
    logging.warn("ACK : %s %s at %s", pubsub_message.message_id, pubsub_message.data, timestamp())
    pubsub_message.ack()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    project = 'google.com:kir-learns-cloud'
    create_topic(project, 'sachin')
    subscriber = pubsub.SubscriberClient()
    subscriber.subscribe("projects/%s/subscriptions/%s"%(project, "sachin"), callback=callback)
    while True:
        time.sleep(10)

* _changelog_ - used on PRs or issues to generate 'more details links in the [changelog](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/develop/CHANGELOG.rst)

* _TAG: ..._ - general organizational labels, usually used on issues but sometimes also on PRs

Also a second commit that adds flexibility to how logging works in SoapClient.

SoapClient has been a module-level log object, set to __name__ (the module name). If you use multiple SoapClient objects, you're either tracing all of them or none of them, which can cause a lot of noise when you only need to see one.

With this change, logging will continue to work this way by default, but there is now a log_id option that can be passed to the object. You can either set it to a string you specify (if you've configured logger manually in your program), or if you set it to False, it will use a logging id unique to that instance of SoapClient.

This allows you to use "trace = True, log_id = False" and have _only_ the SoapClient object you're instantiating perform soap traces. Also, if you pass a custom log_id, you can change it willy nilly after instantiating via calling logging.GetLogger('your_id_here') in your own program.

@oberstet With regards to the two details

a) I actually wanted to get your input on this. Right now the way I wrote it preserves the precedence of the `options` passed in to `session.register` and `session.subscribe`. So if you pass in `options` to one of those methods it overrides what was defined using the decorator. However, I'm not sure this is actually a good thing. If one decorates multiple functions in order to `register`/`subscribe` them and one provides those functions with differing `RegisterOptions` and/or `SubscribeOptions` then is it really appropriate for all those different options to be overriden with the single input to `session.register` or `session.subscribe`. My instinct is no but maybe you guys feel differently?

b) Putting that stuff on the `Pattern` sounds way nicer than using tuples. I merely used tuples so as to keep my additional implementation details separate from the base behaviour. Putting it on the pattern would also mean I could get rid of the changes I had to make to the one test case.

ok, I think this is done done. the main recent changes are

* transpose the `batch_size` and `sequence_length` dimensions when computing the log likelihood to make it easier to work with them
* instead of adding fake `START_TAG` and `END_TAG`s, add separate `start_transition` and `end_transition` parameters (and then make a "fake" transition matrix inside `viterbi_tags`)
* move `viterbi_tags` into the `ConditionalRandomField` module where it belongs
* add much more comprehensive test for the `CRF` module (check the log likelihood is correct, both with and without masking, and add a test that `viterbi_tags` actually finds the most likely tags)
* one of those test revealed that `ConditionalRandomField.forward()` wasn't actually doing the right thing with masking; I think I fixed it correctly.
* update new tutorial to reflect all of these changes

I'm at SciPy 2015 working next to @stefanv<https://github.com/stefanv>. He would like me to refactor scikit-image's ImageCollection to behave like pims objects, backed by SliceableIterable. After some discussion with @JDWarner<https://github.com/JDWarner>, we concluded that the best way to start is to make a very light standalone package that both pims and scikit-image can depend upon, in the pattern of cycler<https://github.com/matplotlib/cycler>, history<https://github.com/Nikea/history>, and other simple utilities. This seems better than copying code and maintaining it in two places.

To be clear, I think pims has a good niche as a user-facing package, and this is not necessarily a step toward pushing pims wholly into scikit-image. It spreads the pims-like interface as a common pattern in the scipy stack.

While this would technically add a new dependency to pims, it wouldn't be a user-facing one: the user would not normally import it, and there will be never installation issues because sliceable_iterable depends only on core Python and six.

Questions:

2. If so, would you be willing to be co-owners of a new sliceable_iterable repo? It is important to scikit-image that there be a couple people with permissions to the repo and PyPI.

It's generally a bad idea to have multiple ways of performing the same task, and I believe that #196 would be a misfeature.

IMO, `globus task wait $(globus delete $src $dst)` is preferable because it clearly delineates the waiting options from the deletion options, and there's no confusion about which behavior is being controlled by a given option. `globus rm` would have to graft together numerous options from `globus delete` and `globus task wait`, and I'm not sure that's a good idea at all.

Similitude to local shell commands is a mirage which we should not necessarily chase. Already, we have to contend with locally interpreted glob patterns being applied to the target endpoint, and other "brokenness" that we aren't prepared to resolve. Making our commands less obviously unique/dissimilar may be very confusing for users.

No, in practice this infinite loop in the Synchronous queue is not the problem. I've added a lot of logs on my fork of the lib (https://github.com/nicodeslandes/ApplicationInsights-Python), and the issue is that for some reason sometimes the App Insights service doesn't answer anything, even after 10 minutes. That cause the web call to block forever, so the `while True:` is not even evaluated.
(By the way the fact that the socket timeout fixes the problem does indeed indicate that the infinite loop is not at fault here).

I haven't tried it, but looking at the code, my guess is that the `AsynchronousSender` will also see the same problem with the web call, as no timeout will be set either in that case. That will result in all the requests to be enqueued in the `AsynchronousQueue`, but never actually sent to the server because the sending thread will be blocked. That might actually be worst, as there won't be any way for the caller to tell that nothing is being sent to the server when that happens.

It seems to me that the proper fix to this issue is to set a timeout (ideally configurable) in the call to `HTTPClient.urlopen()` in the `SenderBase` call. That should fix the problem in both synchronous and asynchronous cases.

If the new execution succeeds (before rollback), then remove the old transaction at the same nonce, and insert the new pending one.

    def try_hard_link(pkgs_dir, prefix, dist):
        src = join(pkgs_dir, dist, 'info', 'index.json')
        dst = join(prefix, '.tmp-%s' % dist)
        assert isfile(src), src
        assert not isfile(dst), dst
        try:
            if not isdir(prefix):
                os.makedirs(prefix)
            _link(src, dst, LINK_HARD)
            # Some file systems, at least BeeGFS, do not support hard-links
            # between files in different directories. Depending on the file system
            # configuration, a symbolic link may be created instead. If a symbolic
            # link is created instead of a hard link, return False.

        except OSError:
            return False

            rm_rf(dst)

The `CONDA_SUBDIR` and `subdir` condarc parameter can now be specified.  Also undocumented, you can specify `CONDA_SUBDIRS` as a string-delimited list and `subdirs` as a list in condarc.  These remain undocumented and experimental for now.  We have plans to expand the power of what we use subdirs for.  Any specific custom implementation might not be completely compatible with what is eventually settled on.  Thus the reason these remain experimental and undocumented.  Some related PRs are https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/4178 and https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/5030.

Actually this doesn't interfere with https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/3708 at all.  In fact https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/3708 is intended to make room exactly for this.  Unfortunately the team is still not in full agreement on ultimate implementation.  See discussion in https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/3739 and https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/4238.

@mbargull Running/compiling Python code in any optimized mode will disable assert statements, therefore asserts should never be used for control flow. Assertions are debug level statements for checking assumptions in a chunk of code.  We shouldn't assume that Python is always running our code in what is effectively a debug mode.  Using assert statements could introduce the silent fail behavior you wanted to avoid.

As for the silent fail potential, a better way would probably be to return NotImplemented if the type check fails.

Actually, I think we're all on the same page.  Misunderstood about the third option as @mcg1969 mentions.  That's the behavior I would intuitively expect too.

so the '..' is actually indicative of using move_to_trash in a way that wasn't anticipated (the folder you trash *not* being a sub-folder of the root prefix).

it's potentially a bad security exploit too, if you get a few '..'s you can get up to the root of the system and start writing files into /usr/lib, for example.

I'm fixing the '..' now, but also keeping the mkdtemp since otherwise we'll run into failures to put things in trash since there's already a folder with the same name in there.

 Now I have a separate instance of Elasticsearch() for each process.

            }
            resp = requests.post(
                url,
                data=data,
                headers=headers
            )
```

                # msrestazure doesn't handle expires_on correctly, so convert it to expires_at if it isn't set already
                if token.get('expires_at') is None:
                    token['expires_at'] = token['expires_on']

                creds = AADTokenCredentials(token=token, resource=token['resource'])
```

Sorry, I should've explained where the above number comes from. 

As part of HMM parameter estimation you need to compute the probability of transitioning from component `i` to component `j` at observation `t`. Therefore, you need a matrix of shape `n_components * n_components * n_observations`. In your particular case the total number of items in a matrix is 

```
>>> 500 * 500 * 56829
14207250000
```

Each element is stored as [double](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Double-precision_floating-point_format) which takes 8 bytes. This give us a total of

```
>>> 500 * 500 * 56829 * 8 / (2**20)  # in Mb
108392.71545410156
```

I'm not sure how to solve this without significantly changing the learning algorithm. Anyway, I'll do a search in the literature and post the results here.

There are at least three signal sets:
1. Request: *send headers* -> [ *send body part* ] -> *finish*.
2. Response: *receive headers* -> [ *receive body part* ] -> *finish*.
3. Connection: *request connection* -> *connection acquired* -> *connection released*. Maybe we need additional signals for DNS resolving and proxy tunneling.

We should have a clean separation between aiohttp developing mode and usage.
In development, I'd like to be as strict as possible.
C Compiler error exception was removed after I spent several hours trying to figure out why my C changes are not visible by tests. The answer was trivial: I had suppressed compilation error.

So restriction to have a properly prepared environment (cython, submodules, whatever) when *hacking aiohttp* is perfectly fine.

Installing aiohttp from PyPI for usage in a project is different.
We have binary wheels for most popular platforms, they work fine.
If a user has a not supported platform `pip` downloads and installs tarball.
The tarball already has cython compiled files (it is a part of our deployment process), no need to install Cython on user's machine.
`setup.py` tries to compile C extensions and falls back to pure python implementation if C compiler is not available. Compilation errors are reported carefully and break the build. We can add verbosity to reported error messages (say, recommend to install `python-dev` package if Python includes are not available) -- but I don't care too much now. Prefer to wait for a problem report first.

We have no pure python environment for testing, that's true. I check it manually after every release.
Tests automation is absent because I don't want to slow down our already not fast Travis bots.
We can do it on nightly builds but again I don't care too much and don't want to invest my own tie to make it work.

Using the Markdown cell (once sanitation issues are solved) addresses 2 of my main concerns:

1. HTML magic (and others like Javascript) places a requirement on the kernel that for many use-cases seem unreasonable and, for certain languages, not practical to implement (i.e  IRkernel/IRkernel#260).
2. From a UX point of view, it is beneficial to have a well defined place to author client side content. Having to type `%%HTML` always felt kludgy.

Having said that... there are so many ways to author client side content that may not fit as nicely in the Markdown cell and might be better suited if we had some level of extensibility around cell types. @bollwyvl brought up https://github.com/pugjs/jade as another alternative to avoid typing HTML. There is always some new flavor of the month.

Also as @rgbkrk hinted at with WYSIWYG comment, the maturation of the Notebook space is going to lead to higher level authoring experiences. Jupyter and the NB format should somehow accommodate for that to avoid being overshadowed by the countless alternatives that are popping up all over.

I understand the importance of the downstream tools (i.e. nbviewer) and the hesitation of an open set of cell types, but as notebooks become platforms for solution development, I think this issue is going to become more and more important to solve.

@omgq That's not the correct way to use Schematics. You want something like this instead:

```python
m = SomeModel({"intt":1, "strt": "abcd"})
...
m.validate()
```

Or, to validate immediately as you load the data:

```python
m = SomeModel({"intt":1, "strt": "abcd"}, validate=True)
```

Or, using the functional API (without instantiating the model):

```python
from schematics.validate import validate

validate(SomeModel, {"intt":1, "strt": "abcd"})
```

Peru uses [docopt](http://docopt.org/) for command line parsing, which actually uses the docstring itself to figure out how to parse arguments, so it should be pretty easy for us to write a nice git-style sub-command help thing without repeating ourselves. There's actually [an example of just that](https://github.com/docopt/docopt#subparsers-multi-level-help-and-huge-applications-like-git) in the docopt repo.

Right now our docopt string is [a big blob in `peru/main.py`](https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/blob/d935d409366f5b7738b367057c6eee04c1e4cd1d/peru/main.py#L17-40), with all the sub-commands handled in one parsing step. As they do in that example, we'll probably want to split that into separate files somehow, and I bet we'll want to use plain text files to hold the docstrings. Writing those text files is definitely something that you could help with without needing to write Python code, though you'd need to be careful to stick to the way docopt expresses things. Maybe take a look at the docopt instructions and see how much sense those make?

I don't know of a widely-used way to generate man pages from docstrings, or to generate man pages and docstrings from some other thing. It seems like using `pip` to install man pages is a little nonstandard too. We might want to punt on man pages then, unless you know of a project that does a really clean job of it.

In other news, #97 was totally my fault, and in the future I don't plan to let `README.md` get out of sync with the latest release. We can probably create a `readme-next` branch or something to hold changes that haven't launched yet.

- Data centers
- Servers
- Links

- DNS keys

How about LE infrastructure help out here by performing a DH param generation with strong primes (4096) continuously, say, one new every hour or so, and providing them as a source for server admins?

LE would be serving a total of more than 8000 different DH primes per year. Admins should be able to choose between (1) DH params from LE, or (2) DH params generated locally. If they choose to get DH params from LE, the script would be provided a set of the last, say, 32 generated params to randomly pick one of them for use.

Another option one could give them is to either (1) do not update DH params ever, (2) automatically fetch/generate new DH params with every new certificate update, (3) automatically fetch/generate new params every *n* days (including server reload after finished generation).

In case someone chooses to generate DH params locally, I’d add an option for specifying a nice level, where applicable, for ensuring that his server isn’t overloaded with just DH params generation. I don’t think it would make much sense to have the DH param bit length as an option.

I actually implemented this with tests (see #281) and I think I understand what @abalkin meant about attractive nuisances. The difference in behavior between an `absolutedelta` and `timedelta` would actually be quite subtle - I was a bit surprised to find that this actually works:

```python
>>> from dateutil.tz import tz
>>> from datetime import datetime, timedelta
>>> dt_edt = datetime(2015, 7, 19, 3, tzinfo=tz.gettz('US/Eastern'))
>>> dt_pdt = datetime(2015, 7, 19, 3, tzinfo=tz.gettz('US/Pacific'))
>>> (dt_pdt - dt_edt) / timedelta(hours=1)
3.0
```

It's a bit more complicated to implement the `absolutedelta` than I thought because it seems that `datetime` will use its own logic for arithmetic if the right hand operand is a `timedelta`. It seems overkill to have a whole `timedelta` pseudo-subclass sitting around just to handle this specific operation.

I haven't given up on the idea entirely, however. I'm thinking this might be something that works better as a convenience *function*, like `absolutedelta.add` and `absolutedelta.subtract`, to at least abstract away the conversion to UTC.

@scls19fr The reason that a dateutil timezone and a pytz timezone representing the same time zone don't resolve as equal is that the concept of equality between timezone objects is not well-defined, and so the `__eq__` method is not defined on either object. By default `__eq__` just checks if two objects are the same object:

```python
>>> class MyClass(object):
>>>     pass
>>> a = MyClass()
>>> b = MyClass()
>>> a == b
False
>>> c = a
>>> a == c
True
```

I think the reason the two datetimes compare equal is that datetimes are considered to be equal if they represent the same actual time (e.g. if you convert them both to UTC and they represent the same moment, they are the same thing, even if their representations are in two different time zones):

```python
>>> from dateutil import tz
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> dt1 = datetime(2015, 11, 1, 9, 45, tzinfo=tz.gettz('America/New_York'))
>>> dt2 = dt1.astimezone(tz.utc())
>>> dt1
datetime.datetime(2015, 11, 1, 9, 45, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/America/New_York'))
>>> dt2
datetime.datetime(2015, 11, 1, 14, 45, tzinfo=tzutc())
>>> dt1 == dt2
True
```

I think both equality behaviors are sane and reasonable and so I don't think anything needs to change here.

Next topic: the StoredAccountInfo class.

The B2 service enforces a rule that each upload URL / auth token pair can be used for only one upload at a time.  This was a recent addition to the [B2 documentation](https://www.backblaze.com/b2/docs/uploading.html).

The StoredAccountInfo class needs to ensure that upload URLs are not shared among multiple processes running at the same time.  I propose that the interface to the class be changed to support a pool of upload URLs for each bucket, and that the threads doing uploading take one, use it, and then put it back in the pool.

Well, `consumes` is specified on the level of method configuration, so if you use apispec to generate that part of swagger.json, you simply specify this in the docstrings:

```python
@app.route('/random')
def random_pet():
    """A cute furry animal endpoint.
    ---
    get:
        description: Get a random pet
        consumes:
            - 'multipart/form-data'
            - 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'

            200:
                description: A pet to be returned
                schema: PetSchema
    """
    pet = get_random_pet()
    return jsonify(PetSchema().dump(pet).data)
```

This is how I produced the user_features matrix: (data is my complete dataframe and customers is a list of unique sorted UserIDs  `customers = list(np.sort(data.UserID.unique()))`

```
def create_user_features(customers, data):
    data = data[["UserID", "Gender", "Age_Group"]].drop_duplicates().reset_index(drop=True)
    sorterIndex = dict(zip(customers, range(len(customers)))) 
    data['User_Rank'] = data['UserID'].map(sorterIndex) 
    data = data.sort_values(by='User_Rank')
    data = data[['Gender', 'Age_Group']]
    data_as_dicts = [dict(r.iteritems()) for _, r in data.iterrows()]
    vectorizer = DictVectorizer()
    user_feat = vectorizer.fit_transform(data_as_dicts)
    user_iden = identity(len(customers), format='csr')
    user_features = sparse.hstack([user_iden, user_feat])
    return user_features
```

Probably because of this nobody parallelized Gromacs analysis tools. RMSF for example also seems I/O bound at least [here][1].

Maybe are namespace packages tricky because every package need to [declare itself as such][2]? This isn't a problem here as we will have only one or two separated packages (`gromacs.analysis` and `gromacs.fileformats`). Regarding analysis plugins we can instead ignore metclassses and just inspect `BasePlugin.__bases__`.  Or yet make them into a different namespace (e.g. `gromacsplugins`) and  create a metclass that [monkey patch][3] analysis plugins automatically into object inside `gromacs` but remove the `BasePlugin`:

```python
class PluginRegister(type):
    def __init__(cls, name, bases, nmspc):
        super(Plugin, cls).__init__(name, bases, nmspc)
        if not hasattr(cls, 'registry'):
            cls.registry = set()
        cls.registry.add(cls)
        cls.registry -= set([BasePlugin])

class BasePlugin(object):
    __metaclass__ = PluginRegister
```

I vote for namespace packages!

[1]: https://github.com/gromacs/gromacs/blob/master/src/gromacs/gmxana/gmx_rmsf.cpp#L394
[2]: http://stackoverflow.com/a/1676069/199332
[3]: http://python-3-patterns-idioms-test.readthedocs.io/en/latest/Metaprogramming.html#example-self-registration-of-subclasses

I'm afraid there's a bit too many variables left in this request. Z3 doesn't know how to learn "hard" or "soft", it will resolve whatever conflict it hits, which depends on the variable and phase selection heuristics (and more). Or, in other words, it already learns as "hard" as possible every time and it will never give up because it can't "learn hard enough".

The differences in performance that you're seeing are most likely due to the order of quantifier instantiation. Sometimes Z3 is lucky and it picks just about the right instance for the crucial quantifier, and at other times it will instantiate a thousand irrelevant quantifiers until it finally hits the right one. Simply changing the order of assertions, variable declarations, or the random seed, frequently makes the difference. (Shameless plug: [this](https://github.com/Wintersteiger/z3/commits/quant-exp) branch is able to record an SMT2-format, readable, and replayable quantifier instantiation log that you can play around with. Set `smt.search_log=filename.smt2` and replace `(check-sat)` with `(replay "filename.smt2")`. Then remove all the irrelevant ones... )

Obviously, those are all undesirable properties for many applications. Suggestions and new ideas that maintain performance while improving predictability are most welcome though!

Also note that lots of "independent" lemmas also clog up the memory, so you don't necessarily want to collect and keep all irrelevant lemmas (or quantifier instances) forever. 

(Since your example file contains `(set-option :timeout 0)`: Combining timeouts with a search algorithm makes everything non-deterministic, so now you don't even have to change the random seed to make it fail! Use rlimits (`(set-option :rlimit <n>)` and similar) for a deterministic way of resource bounding.)

@neilsummers I'll do this today unless you've been working on it.

1. I think this is a bit vague, which parts in particular do you think should change to make things more object-oriented? If you're talking about `get_output` specifically, this used to be a `Layer` method until we figured out that sometimes you want to compute the output of multiple layers, and you want to ensure that they share computations as much as possible while doing so. That necessarily meant that it had to live outside the `Layer` class, because it can also be applied to collections of layers.

iirc we debated keeping `layer.get_output(...)` as a shortcut for `get_output(layer, ...)`, but decided against it because then people would do things like `[l.get_output(...) for l in layers]`, which is bad. It's better in the long term if there is a single convention for using `get_output()` which is the same for single layers and collections of layers.

2. `get_output(theLayer).shape` really shouldn't be time-consuming, since it is a symbolic expression. It should be very fast unless you have a network with thousands of layers, and Theano starts to have trouble building up the graph. Note also that in many cases, Theano should be smart enough to realise that you only want the shape of this thing, and avoid doing the actual computation. I say "should", I don't actually know to which extent that is true. But if it isn't true, what should happen is that Theano's shape inference is improved -- I don't think changing Lasagne to replicate this functionality is the right solution, to be honest.

3. I'm not sure if I agree that being able to filter by layer names (even with regexps) is useful, isn't this a problem you can easily solve in Python instead and then use `[l.get_params(..) for l in layers]`? I think the additional convenience probably doesn't warrant the additional complexity here (which is something [we are always very wary of](http://lasagne.readthedocs.org/en/latest/user/development.html#philosophy).

You can already set custom tags on parameters if you like. I'm not sure if I agree that it would be useful to do this by passing a tag dictionary to the layer at construction time. Typically you would want to set tags on individual parameter variables, not on all the parameter variables of a given layer. And if you do, it's as easy as `layer.params[layer.W].add('tag')`. Maybe a helper method would make this a little easier to remember?

> Now the grid ends at `width_f - 1`, so does that mean we should mirror about `2 * (width_f - 1)`?

Well, there's a clear definition for the `mirror` mode at http://stackoverflow.com/a/22670830. My solution above might be the `reflect` mode instead, I only briefly tried it with a picture of a parrot, which was nice to look at, but not the best choice for scrutiny ;)
Let's just go through the formula for a toy image of three pixels. We would want the grid positions `-1, 0, 1` to be mapped to `0, 1, 2` (since the grid spans from the left corner of the first pixel (0) to the left corner of the third pixel (2)), this is catered for by the conversion to `x`. Grid positions `2, 3, 4, 5, 6` will be mapped to pixel positions `3, 4, 5, 6, 7`. We want the border mode code for `mirror` to map this to `1, 0, 1, 2, 1` (`reflect` would be `2, 1, 0, 0, 1` instead).
My solution above was `min(x mod 2*width, 2*width - 1 - x)`. With `width = 3` and `x = [3, 4, 5, 6, 7]`, this would give `[2, 1, 0, -1, -2]`. So that's wrong, the second part needs a `mod` as well. With `min(x mod 6, (5 - x) mod 6)` we'll have `reflect`:
```
>>> x = np.arange(12)
>>> np.minimum(x % 6, (5 - x) % 6)
array([0, 1, 2, 2, 1, 0, 0, 1, 2, 2, 1, 0])
```
The correct solution for `mirror` would be:
```
>>> np.minimum(x % 4, (4 - x) % 4)
array([0, 1, 2, 1, 0, 1, 2, 1, 0, 1, 2, 1])
```
Do you want to continue with this? (I'd suggest to not include `reflect`, as before, even if the two are both easy to implement now that we're in pixel space. I think `reflect` is usually not what you want, and including both makes it confusing for users.)

> Should we have these types in a separate package? Or, should the core stuff be in core? What's the common convention?

My personal take on this is that the records should be in core. The `@nteract/types` package was created for us to unify a lot of the types that were being used all over the place where there's a general type (jupyter messages, notebook format bits, ...). I think it makes sense to actually move the records bits from `@nteract/types` to `@nteract/core`. The raw kernelspecs structure (as exists on disk) is probably wise inside of `@nteract/types`.

Modifying the PC register with a breakpoint will not work due to the way the execution process works - a block of code is lifted and executed at a time.

The correct approach here is to add a hook (consult the docs for how to do this) that does nothing, on top of the 4 bytes of the BL instruction. The hook will change the way the instruction-lifting process works so it will stop before the BL instruction, then your hook will execute, doing nothing, and then it will resume 4 bytes later on the MOV instruction.

```
%%local

```
%%local
import declarativewidgets as declwidgets
declwidgets.init()

Hi,
Are you using `example.hpp` and its `window` class for the rendering loop?
If so then please note that the `while (app) { /*...*/ }`  both swaps and clears the screen buffer each iteration, this is because `window` performs all of the OpenGL code inside its boolean casting operator:

https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/a8b8567f3859f7c1cf1ff557fa03b1d22b322376/examples/example.hpp#L177-L197

This means that if you have some `continue` inside the loop, which continues to the next iteration before "uploading" the next image, the loop will render a blank frame and not the previous image.

If this is not the case, please share some of your code so we can try to assist.

heh, well, it's not that easy. Git stores all text files with unix endings. If you say that a file should have CRLF endings, then it still stores those files in the repository with unix endings---git just automatically does conversions to/from CRLF when you checkout.

So all of these files already have CRLF in the git repository. Changing them to have the CRLF attribute means that, in the actual back end, all of those CRLF endings need to be converted to be unix format. Otherwise you get tons of stuff in your "git status" that says you have changed it but you haven't really.

Sorry @moonshoes87 that change was me. I was trying to run all the tests using `python -m unittest discover` and was getting path and permissions errors galore as it was trying to read/write to protected drive space (/usr/ to be exact, which makes sense in the context of a pip install). This also just seemed like the wrong logic for the tests, theoretically at least it should find the pmagpy directory somehow. I assumed that was what pmagpy.check_updates.get_pmag_dir was for and started replacing WD=sys.prefix with calls to the get_pmag_dir funciton.

__labelA__ , ID, sentence1

I'll just dump core here. You may already know this stuff.

I found `shlex` to be very effective in the latest tab completion code I added.
Take a look at `tokens_for_completion()`. You could probably take the middle part of the function that parses out redirection/pipes and turn it into a common function that your tokenizer and the completion tokenizer use. That block of code works similar to how punctuation characters are treated in `shlex` when using Python 3.6 or greater: "any run of these characters (considered punctuation characters) is returned as a single token"

Just using the basic shlex.split() method was enough. It splits only on whitespace and properly handles quoted tokens. These settings don't restrict `shlex.wordchars` to ASCII and therefore allows us to support utf-8 command line arguments. If we only plan on supporting # for comments, then you can just set `comments` to True when calling split. I'm not sure why C-style comments were ever supported in cmd2. Continuing to do so will require custom parsing of the `shlex` tokens, which isn't hard, but its weird supporting C-style comments in a Python based CLI.

Your tokenizer function would be simpler than `tokens_for_completion()` since you can just print an error if the command line has an unclosed quote.

Example: `bind *:443 ssl crt domain1.pem crt domain2.pem strict-sni`

> I have a hunch that this is related to a weird Include or IncludeOptional statement somewhere in the configuration.

Bingo.  Because this is a standardized config generated by Puppet valid for multiple operating systems, apache2.conf contains:
```
IncludeOptional [c]onf.modules.d/*.conf
IncludeOptional mods-enabled/*.load
IncludeOptional mods-enabled/*.conf
```
What's happening here is that this is a valid (if overly fancy) trick that works in Apache itself because [c]onf.modules.d forces a regular expression parse, which prevents it from failing in an IncludeOptional when the directory doesn't exist... but this is breaking an assumption either in Augeas or in the parser.

@McLive, it should if the set of domains you're requesting is a superset of the domains already found in your existing certificate. For example, if you have a certificate for `example.com` and `other.example.com` found in `/etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com`, running:
```
certbot -d example.com -d yet.another.example.com --expand
```
will result in `/etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com-0001`.

If, however, you include all existing domains as well as the new ones like so:
```
certbot -d example.com -d other.example.com -d yet.another.example.com --expand
```
this will expand the existing certificate.

If this isn't the behavior your experiencing, please open a new issue with more details to help track that problem.

NOTE: If you provide a set of domains which is a superset of more than one certificate, the one that gets expanded is undefined. See #2128 for more info.

For those that need support for Windows because they do not have any Linux machines available, one solution might be to purchase a thumb drive (8gb or larger) or a DVD, download your favorite Linux distro, and look into directions on that distro's site about how to set up a [Live Disc](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Live_CD). For instance, [Ubuntu LiveCD](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/LiveCD). You should then be able to, following CertBot documentation, generate and export your certificates and then add them to your Windows host (or anywhere else) manually. Unfortunately, this solution does not support automated renewal, but is a good temporary work-around if you do not have the money to drop on a new dedicated Linux box or are just looking to do one-time cert generation for testing purposes.

First, it is the hottest stuff in the python world, so even if we are not using it with sos, we should learn it.

Second, sos on windows is not working because we use subprocesses a lot and windows is really bad at python multi-processing (basically each process is new, need to start a python interpreter, load sos and all its environments from scratch). I **hope** asyncio can save us there.

Third, the execution of DAG is currently implemented by a simple process-status array. It works but might not stand the abuse of large workflows and we might run into IO problems if we are not doing async IO.

From the doc: https://sassoftware.github.io/sas_kernel/install.html

    For OSX, the only supported configuration is an IOM connection.

In my particular case,

1. Figure out the location of configuration file using
    ```
    python -c 'import saspy;print(saspy.SAScfg)'
    ```
    which is something like `/Users/bpeng1/anaconda/lib/python3.5/site-packages/saspy/sascfg.py` for anaconda python3.

2. Log on to the remote server and find the SAS Deployment Manger Java Files, where are located at somewhere like `/usr/local/data/SASHome/SASDeploymentManager/9.3/products/deploywiz__93540__prt__xx__sp0__1/deploywiz/`, **copy the files to the local machine**, then modify the cfg file with something like:

```
SAS_config_names=['iom']

SAS_config_options = {'lock_down': True}

cpL  =  "/path/to/sas.svc.connection.jar"
cpL += ":/path/to/log4j.jar"
cpL += ":/path/to/sas.security.sspi.jar"
cpL += ":/path/to/sas.core.jar"
cpL += ":/Users/bpeng1/anaconda/lib/python3.5/site-packages/saspy/java/saspyiom.jar"

iom = {'java'  : '/usr/bin/java',
            'iomhost'   : 'linux_server_URL',
            'iomport'   : 8591,
            'encoding'  : 'latin1',
            'classpath' : cpL,
           'authkey': 'linux_server',
            }           
```

3. Create an authkey file (e.g. `~/.authkey`) with username and password
    ```
    linux_server  user XXX password XXXX
     ```
4. Test the configuration file with command

    ```
    python -c 'import saspy;saspy.SASsession()'
    ```

You will see something like "SAS Connection failed.". This is where we need to start the SAS server from the server, which is explained [here](http://support.sas.com/rnd/itech/doc9/admin_oma/sasserver/startserv/sp_suunx.html).
It still makes some sense to convert remote targets if possible because we are getting `remote(filename)` which is conceptually still a `path`.

Thanks @robertnishihara and @elibol for raising all the valid points, we are getting closer to a good solution!

We are now having two questions under discussion:
1. Driver death detection.
The main problem in previous solution is it cannot handle driver hang and machine failure, I believe we have to solve it in production level system. To deal with driver hand case, we cannot rely on the driver itself to notify its death; to deal with machine failure, we cannot rely on the local node manager. Heartbeat to store (Redis for now) seems to be the only choice as far as I can see. GIL is indeed a valid concern. I guess we can solve it in this way: adjust the detection policy, for example set heartbeat period to 5 seconds, and consider a driver dead if lose heartbeat in 60 seconds. Given Python has a fixed interval for a thread to release GIL, see [here](https://realpython.com/python-gil/). 
2. Redis clean up.
In order to improve the perf we introduce a set that stores all the keys of resources belong to a driver. Yes, this will double the Redis command number. But in the previous solution, we scan the all the tables, get each item out and check if it belongs to a specific driver, I believe it is much more heavier. Note that, all will be integrated in custom Redis module so only one command is called from the client's perspective. 
As for potential store replacement, this is a good point to consider about. But since all existing interfaces are in custom module, we will have re-write this part if we want to move to new stores, and clean up is just a small part of this effort, right?
Theoretically, it make sense to wrapper the clean up actions for a driver into one command inside custom Redis module, since it saves redis connections compared with doing separately from a client. The question here is where do we trigger this command, or a client (monitor process) or inside Redis.

I haven't considered about sharding carefully, I will find a time to think about it.

The socks5 option of the `--https-proxy` parameter and the twitch plugin are working fine. Btw, since the twitch plugin doesn't make any regular http requests, you can omit the `--http-proxy` parameter.

Just a very simple bash script to see that Streamlink is actually using the socks5 proxy server for all of its requests:

```bash
streamlink --https-proxy "socks5://address:port" URL STREAM &
PID=$!
netstat -tp | grep "$PID/python"
```

> In my VLC player, it was still showing my IP

It'll only "show your IP address in your player" if you're using the `--player-passthrough` parameter with a value of `http` or `rtmp`, which tells Streamlink to run a **local proxy server** to which the player will connect to instead of reading the stream data from `stdin` when not using this parameter. If you're using `--player-passthrough=hls` (which you should not when using a proxy server), Streamlink will launch the player with the URL of Twitch's HLS playlist as the first parameter, so that it has to download the stream by itself instead of reading from Streamlink's (passthrough) proxy server.

Can you post the whole command (without explicit proxy server addresses)? Please also make sure that your config file doesn't contain anything special that may interfere here...

@truanifan -- CURL is super easy.  Just download a binary build for your OS.  Then just open a command prompt and type:

curl "http://sitename.com/path/master.m3u8" <- the full exact HLS endpoint you would have entered into streamlink.  (If your URL uses HTTPS you might need to use it in the form of curl -k "https://sitename.com/path/master.m3u8" to get it to bypass the certificate check)


The output returned to your command prompt window will be the HLS manifest which will be explicit in resolutions and bitrates.  Each resolution/bitrate combination will have its own corresponding .m3u8 link.  Just copy the particular stream link that you care about and use the individual bitrate/resolution manifest m3u8 URL as your streamlink input.  The only difference is that instead of using the hlsvariant:// protocol, you will just use the hls:// protocol.


Super easy.

Well, if `__del__` doesn't close the fds, what else can it do?

Oh, here's another fun issue to keep in mind: [`TextIOWrapper` objects are not thread safe](https://docs.python.org/3/library/io.html#multi-threading). This means that if we naively `wrap_file(sys.std...)`, then the resulting object is unsafe to call from multiple tasks simultaneously. Which is worrisome because it's a global object. Perhaps some locking is in order.

The globalness of the standard descriptors causes several problems, actually. If we set them non-blocking, then it's not just other processes that get messed up, it's also naive calls to `print` or similar within our process. Obviously `print` is not a great thing to be calling all over the place in a robust app that wants to make sure it never blocks, but for things like debugging or test output it's pretty useful. `pdb.set_trace` is another example of why we might want to keep stdin/stdout working in blocking mode.

... And actually this is also trickier than it might seem, because the thread safety issue also applies between the main thread and worker threads, i.e. even if `trio.stdout` has some locking inside it so that all calls that go through it serialized, then they can still race with direct accesses to `sys.stdout`. It's possible that we could avoid this by using two different TextIOWrapper objects pointed at the same underlying BufferedIO object, which creates different possibilities for corrupt output when both are used at the same time, but at least internal data structures would survive.

Anyway, one thing this makes clear is that the decision to use the standard fds for programmatic purposes is really not something to take lightly – if you're going to do it then the whole program needs to agree on how.

Oh, I just remembered another fun thing about stdin: trying to read from it can cause your whole program to get suspended (SIGTSTP).

Stream details are indeed fetched directly from Kinesis:  https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/master/localstack/services/dynamodbstreams/dynamodbstreams_api.py#L67

Shard information is returned as-is back to the user:  https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/master/localstack/services/dynamodbstreams/dynamodbstreams_api.py#L70

I seems like a straightforward fix to unwrap shard ids to/from Kinesis.  For example, given the shard ids returned from Kinesis the emulated dynamodbstreams service can return `$KinesisShardID-$RandomAsciiString`.  The remaining operations then can parse out `-$RandomAsciiString` and pass only `$KinesisShardID` to underlying Kinesis operations.

Hi @linkerzhang 
I think a significant portion of this patch is for ensuring correctness of execution. I used a bad example as introduction and please allow me to edit:

I plan to have support for using this feature to ensure that tensor operations are performed to the correct dimensions/axes. For instance, a "strict mode" can be introduced and disabled by default that ensures reasonable operation definition (for instance, issue warning on concat of differing standard denotation types); spatial pooling on channel dimension.

This also adds to our tensorflow users' confidence that they are feeding in tensors of the right order.

Reading this PR made me also realize that we are mixing up the semantics of `GraphProto`. Let me explain.

Remember that `GraphProto` can be embedded *within* a `GraphProto` via the `g`  attribute type. But does it make sense to respecify things like `ir_version` inside a nested `GraphProto`? No. So this is a pretty clear indication that we messed things up.

It seems like there should be a minimal `GraphProto` that doesn't have all of this extra info, and then a *true* top-level struct, like `OnnxProto`, which contains version info, extra metadata, and then the actual graph.

What do people think?

The outputs of all layers can be iterated as follow:
```python

```

The code has this problem when there are multiple segmentation pathways.
Currently, it try to list all paths then find the shortest (=maximal
matching).
It does this stupidly by go depth-first (recursively) then rank them all.
I wrote a version that go breath-first and find just the shortest path,
also faster around 7% in my test case.
The code is somewhat longer, so for readibility sake, I choose the slower
version.
But for your ‘สสสสสสสสสสส’ case, it will surely be much much faster.

You can see the faster version here.
https://colab.research.google.com/drive/14Ibg-ngZXj15RKwjNwoZlOT32fQBOrBx
Look at onecut_bfs() which call bfs_paths()
It’s my first time doing code profiling, so I don’t have much experience in
optimizing for speed.

--

The error means that the build (i.e. `pip install`), as we ship sdists on PyPI only, occurred on a machine that used glibc 2.14 or greater, but was run on a machine that had a glibc version older than 2.14. IOW it was built on a newer version of Linux than you are running it on. The solution would be to build on the version of Linux you intend to run it on, but for various reasons that may be more complicated. One option might be to try the conda-forge binary for `numcodecs`, which works with glibc 2.12 or newer. If that doesn't work, we can discuss other options.
Also you should have admin access to the repo now.
